subject: ap-chemistry
title: Acid Concentration by Titration (Buret Reading)
stimulus:
A student is given a 25.0 mL sample of a solution of an unknown monoprotic acid and asked to determine the
concentration of the acid by titration. The student uses a standardized solution of 0.110 M NaOH(aq), a buret, a
flask, an appropriate indicator, and other laboratory equipment necessary for the titration.

parts.prompt:
The images below show the buret before the titration begins (below left) and at the end point (below right).
What should the student record as the volume of NaOH(aq) delivered to the flask?
parts.scoring_notes: Volume delivered = final reading − initial reading. From the buret images: initial reading = 5.65 mL, final reading = 37.30 mL. Volume NaOH = 37.30 − 5.65 = 31.65 mL.

scoring_points.description: For the correct volume difference read from buret images: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Volume NaOH delivered = final buret reading − initial buret reading = 37.30 − 5.65 = 31.65 mL. Buret readings are read at the bottom of the meniscus, to the nearest 0.01 mL.

wrong_examples: Using only the final reading (37.30 mL) — must subtract the initial reading.
37.3 or 5.6 without the tenths digit — buret readings are reported to 0.01 mL precision.
common_traps: Volume delivered = final reading − initial reading. Burets are read to ±0.01 mL and report two decimal places.

parts.prompt:
Based on the given information and your answer to part (a), determine the value of the concentration of the
acid that should be recorded in the student's lab report.

scoring_points.description: For correct moles of NaOH used: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{NaOH}) = (0.110\text{ M})(0.03165\text{ L}) = 3.482\times10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
(Convert 31.65 mL to 0.03165 L.)

wrong_examples: Using 31.65 directly without converting to liters.

scoring_points.description: For the correct acid concentration: 1 point
wrong_examples: Dividing by 31.65 instead of 0.0250 L — divide by volume of acid solution (25.0 mL = 0.0250 L).
common_traps: At equivalence: $M_a V_a = M_b V_b$ for 1:1 stoichiometry. Divide moles of base by volume of acid.

parts.prompt:
In a second trial, the student accidentally added more NaOH(aq) to the flask than was needed to reach the
end point, and then recorded the final volume. Would this error increase, decrease, or have no effect on the
calculated acid concentration for the second trial? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Increase. If more NaOH is added than the equivalence point requires, the recorded volume of NaOH is larger than the true equivalence volume. A larger $V$(NaOH) → larger calculated moles of acid → larger calculated [acid]. The experimental concentration will be overestimated.

scoring_points.description: For 'increase' with reasoning about overcounting moles of NaOH: 1 point

required_elements: Recording a volume larger than needed to reach the equivalence point overestimates moles of NaOH used, which overestimates moles of acid and therefore [acid].
alternatives.correct_example: Increase. The student recorded too much NaOH volume (past the equivalence point). In the calculation $[\text{acid}] = M_{\text{NaOH}} V_{\text{NaOH}} / V_{\text{acid}}$, the recorded $V_{\text{NaOH}}$ is too large, giving an overestimated number of moles of NaOH (and therefore acid), resulting in a calculated concentration higher than the actual value.

wrong_examples: Decrease — adding more NaOH records a larger volume, which gives a higher (not lower) calculated concentration.
No effect — the calculation directly uses the recorded NaOH volume.
common_traps: The calculated [acid] = (M × V$_{NaOH}$) / V$_{acid}$. An overrecorded V$_{NaOH}$ inflates the numerator → inflated [acid].
